narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 95% chance that you would not pass based on your academic and background profile. This high predicted risk of failure triggered the rejection to help the university maintain strong bar passage rates for its graduates. The model's decision was driven primarily by your class rank in your third year of undergrad, which fell in the bottom decile—far below the top-half average where most students who pass the bar landed. This stood out as the strongest factor pushing the prediction toward failure, signaling to the model a potential challenge in sustaining strong performance through law school and beyond. Next in influence was your family's middle-income bracket, which, while common overall, appears less frequently among bar passers compared to those from upper-middle or high-income homes, nudging the odds further against success in the model's eyes. Your first-year class rank in the second decile also contributed toward this unfavorable outcome, again trailing the much higher averages seen in successful bar takers. In contrast, students typically admitted have third-year ranks around the sixth decile, first-year ranks near the top, and family incomes skewed toward upper-middle levels, putting your profile notably below those benchmarks. Your LSAT score of 31.5 and undergrad GPA of 2.6, while not the top influences here, aligned with patterns the model associates with higher failure risk when combined with lower ranks. Being a full-time student is a positive commonality with most who pass, but it wasn't enough to offset the key drags. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 37, the average for bar passers, and strengthening your academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program to improve your class standing equivalents. Building a narrative around overcoming these metrics, perhaps with letters highlighting your resilience as a Black woman from a middle-income background pursuing full-time studies, could help in reapplying to other schools with more holistic reviews. With targeted improvements, you can shift your profile toward the accepted students' norms and boost your bar passage odds.
